content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_004_page_001.jpg, 1TH_004_page_002.jpg

---

**4:1** Finally then, brothers, we ask you and urge you in the Lord Jesus, that just as you received from us how you ought to walk and to please God — as indeed you are already doing — that you abound still more.

**4:2** For you know what instructions we gave you through the Lord Jesus.

**4:3** For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that you abstain from sexual immorality,

**4:4** that each of you know how to possess his own vessel in sanctification and honor,

**4:5** not in the passion of desire, like the Gentiles who do not know God,

**4:6** that no one transgress and exploit his brother in this matter, because the Lord is an avenger concerning all these things, just as we told you beforehand and solemnly testified.

**4:7** For God did not call us to uncleanness but to sanctification.

**4:8** Therefore the one who rejects this does not reject man but God, who also gives his Holy Spirit to you.

**4:9** Now concerning brotherly love, you have no need for anyone to write to you, for you yourselves are taught by God to love one another;

**4:10** for indeed you do this toward all the brothers throughout Macedonia. But we urge you, brothers, to abound still more,

**4:11** and to aspire to live quietly, and to attend to your own affairs, and to work with your own hands, just as we instructed you,

**4:12** so that you may walk honorably toward those outside, and may have need of nothing.

**4:13** But we do not want you to be uninformed, brothers, concerning those who have fallen asleep, so that you may not grieve as the rest who have no hope.

**4:14** For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, so also God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep through Jesus.

**4:15** For this we say to you by the word of the Lord, that we who are living, who remain until the coming of the Lord, shall by no means precede those who have fallen asleep.

**4:16** For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a commanding shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trumpet of God; and the dead in Christ shall rise first.

**4:17** Then we who are living, who remain, shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air; and so we shall always be with the Lord.

**4:18** Therefore comfort one another with these words.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter divides into two major sections: Paul's exhortation on holy living (vv. 1-12) and his teaching on the fate of believers who have died (vv. 13-18). The eschatological passage in vv. 13-18 is among the most theologically consequential in the Pauline corpus, and the council's deliberations were especially careful here.

**On sanctification language (vv. 3-8):** All three translators agreed on the core Greek vocabulary. SOPHIA chose "sanctification" over PNEUMA's "set apart" because the Greek AGIASMOS is a substantive noun appearing as a theological term, and "sanctification" preserves its weight in formal discourse while remaining intelligible. EMET's "holiness" (a valid alternative) was set aside because "sanctification" better captures the processual aspect of the Greek term in this context.

**On "vessel" (v. 4):** All three translators preserved the ambiguity of SKEUOS. EMET and LOGOS both rendered it "vessel"; PNEUMA interpreted it as "body." SOPHIA retains "vessel" because the ambiguity is present in the Greek and should be preserved for the reader. The metaphor resists premature resolution.

**On "transgress and exploit" (v. 6):** EMET and LOGOS used "defraud" for PLEONEKTEIN; PNEUMA used "exploits." SOPHIA adopted "exploit" as capturing the predatory dimension of the Greek verb (grasping more than one's due) more forcefully than "defraud," which can suggest financial fraud too narrowly. The pairing "transgress and exploit" preserves both verbs' distinct semantic fields.

**On the eschatological passage (vv. 13-18):** This is the most theologically sensitive section. Key decisions:

- **"Fallen asleep" (vv. 13-15):** All three translators preserved Paul's sleep metaphor for death. SOPHIA concurs: the metaphor is theologically load-bearing — sleep implies awakening, which is the heart of Paul's argument. To substitute "died" would flatten the rhetoric.

- **"Through Jesus" (v. 14):** The phrase DIA TOU IESOU in Sinaiticus is syntactically ambiguous. It may modify "fallen asleep" (those who died in connection with Jesus) or "will bring" (God will bring them through Jesus). EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all connected it with "fallen asleep." SOPHIA follows this consensus, rendering "those who have fallen asleep through Jesus," as this attachment has stronger syntactical support in the manuscript word order.

- **"By the word of the Lord" (v. 15):** EMET and LOGOS rendered EN LOGO KURIOU literally. PNEUMA expanded to "on the Lord's own authority." SOPHIA preserves the literal rendering, as Paul's exact source (a saying of the earthly Jesus? a prophetic word? a direct revelation?) remains debated, and the literal phrase leaves this open.

- **"Commanding shout" (v. 16):** LOGOS and PNEUMA both chose "commanding shout" for KELEUSMATI; EMET rendered it simply "command." SOPHIA adopted "commanding shout" because the Greek noun denotes an audible order — a military cry or captain's command — and both the authoritative and acoustic dimensions should be conveyed.

- **"Caught up" (v. 17):** All three translators agreed on "caught up" for HARPAGESOMETHA. The verb HARPAZO implies forceful seizure — divine initiative snatching believers upward. "Caught up" is both faithful to the Greek passive voice and established in English usage. The Latin Vulgate's RAPIEMUR gave rise to the theological term "rapture," but the English rendering stands independently.

- **"To meet the Lord" (v. 17):** The Greek EIS APANTESIN carries a technical Hellenistic sense: a civic delegation going out to escort a visiting dignitary back into the city. This nuance — that the meeting is not a departure but a welcoming procession — is significant for eschatological interpretation but cannot be fully captured in a single English phrase. SOPHIA renders it plainly as "to meet" and records the nuance here.

**On the article before "Jesus" (v. 14):** EMET noted that Sinaiticus reads DIA TOU IESOU with the article before the name — a distinctive Sinaiticus feature. SOPHIA follows the manuscript as read, though the article does not materially affect English rendering.

**On "gives" vs. "gave" (v. 8):** Sinaiticus reads the present participle DIDONTA ("the one giving"), not the aorist DONTA ("the one who gave"). All three translators rendered this as present tense. SOPHIA preserves this: God's bestowal of the Spirit is presented as ongoing, not a completed past action.

**On inclusive language (vv. 1, 10, 13):** PNEUMA rendered ADELPHOI as "brothers and sisters." EMET and LOGOS retained "brothers." SOPHIA uses "brothers" as the formal rendering of ADELPHOI while acknowledging that Paul's usage was almost certainly inclusive of the entire community. The Greek term is masculine in form but communal in function.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
